mapQuestions.question: Which continent is the UK on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, just off the north-west coast 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What four countries make up the United Kingdom?
mapQuestions.answer: England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of the UK?
mapQuestions.answer: London. It is also the capital of England. Edinburgh is the Scottish capital, Cardiff the Welsh capital, and Belfast the Northern Irish capital.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in the UK?
mapQuestions.answer: Ben Nevis in Scotland, 1,345 metres. Tiny compared to the Himalayas, but plenty steep enough.

mapQuestions.question: Why do British people say "Great Britain" sometimes and "United Kingdom" other times?
mapQuestions.answer: Great Britain is the big island that includes England, Scotland and Wales. The United Kingdom adds Northern Ireland to that. Both terms are used to mean roughly the same thing, but they are not identical.

foods.name: Fish and chips
foods.description: White fish (usually cod or haddock) deep-fried in crispy batter, served with thick-cut chips, mushy peas, and lashings of salt and vinegar. Best eaten out of paper at the seaside.

foods.name: Sunday roast
foods.description: Roast meat (beef, lamb, chicken or pork), Yorkshire puddings, roast potatoes, vegetables, stuffing and gravy. Eaten on Sunday lunchtime, ideally in a pub by a fire.

foods.name: Full English breakfast
foods.description: Bacon, sausage, baked beans, mushrooms, grilled tomato, fried egg, black pudding, hash brown, toast. Often called a "fry-up". Glorious, ridiculous, and a cure for everything.

foods.name: Cornish pasty
foods.description: A folded shortcrust pastry parcel filled with beef, potato, onion and swede (rutabaga). Originally a portable lunch for Cornish tin miners, who would hold it by the thick crust crimped along the edge.

foods.name: Yorkshire pudding
foods.description: A puffy, hollow, golden bowl of batter (flour, egg, milk), baked in hot fat until it rises like a balloon. Eaten with roast beef and gravy. Northerners take this very seriously.

foods.name: Scone with clotted cream and jam
foods.description: A scone (a soft round cake) split open and loaded with thick clotted cream and strawberry jam. In Devon they put cream on first then jam. In Cornwall, jam first then cream. People argue about this.

scavengerItems.label: A red telephone box (Britain has fewer and fewer, but they still pop up in villages)
A bright red postbox with the royal cypher on it (E II R or C III R)
A traditional pub with a wooden hanging sign painted with a coat of arms or animal
A field full of sheep (the UK has more sheep than people in some areas)
A small stone village church with a graveyard around it
A castle, or the ruins of one (the UK has thousands)
A cottage with a thatched (straw) roof
A robin (small bird with a red breast) in a hedge
A road sign with the place name in both Welsh and English (if you are in Wales)
A blue plaque on a building marking where someone famous lived
A piece of an old Roman wall or a Roman mosaic (England has loads)

geoMatches.description: The capital. Big Ben, the Tower of London, the Tube, royal parks, museums, and over 300 languages spoken in the streets.
In the far north of Scotland. Wild mountains, deep lochs (including Loch Ness), bagpipes, kilts, shaggy Highland cattle.
Wales has 1,400km of coast with castles every few miles, the only nation with a 870-mile path that walks all the way around the whole country.
In the north-west of England. Steep stone-walled hills, deep blue lakes, the Wordsworth and Beatrix Potter country.
The far south-west of England, sticking out into the Atlantic. Surfing beaches, fishing villages, Cornish pasties, and the warmest weather in the UK.
On the north Northern Irish coast. 40,000 hexagonal columns of basalt rock stepping into the sea. Made by an ancient volcano (or, the legends say, by a giant called Finn McCool).

senses.smell: Rain on warm tarmac, fresh-cut grass, woodsmoke from pub fires, sausages cooking in a B&B at 8am, salty sea air, sheep in a hill field, jasmine on a summer hedge…
senses.hear: "Alright?" as a greeting, seagulls over a chip shop, an accent that changes every 30 miles, sheep across a valley, church bells on a Sunday, a kettle whistling…
senses.taste: Salty chips with vinegar, hot tea with milk, strawberry jam on warm scones, mint sauce with lamb, brown sauce on a fry-up, the buttery flake of a Cornish pasty…
senses.feel: Wind on a cliff edge, soft Welsh rain, cold North Sea water on bare feet, the weight of a wool jumper, mud on your boots, a wool blanket on a chilly evening…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Does the Loch Ness Monster actually exist?
stories.body: Loch Ness is a long deep lake in the Scottish Highlands, 37km long and up to 230m deep, holding more freshwater than every lake in England and Wales combined. Locals have told stories of a monster in it for over 1,500 years, but the modern legend really kicked off in 1933 when a couple driving past saw "an enormous animal rolling and plunging" in the loch. The next year, a London doctor took a photo of what looked like a long-necked creature, which became the most famous image of Nessie ever. Decades later, the photo was confessed to be a hoax (a model neck stuck on a toy submarine). Scientists have searched the loch with sonar and underwater cameras and found nothing big enough. But Nessie still gets the occasional sighting, and the Loch Ness gift shops do roaring trade. People want her to be real.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Why do you think people still want to believe in the Loch Ness Monster, even when scientists say there is nothing there?

stories.question: Who built Stonehenge, and why?
stories.body: On a windy plain in southern England stand 30 enormous stones arranged in a circle, the biggest ones around 4 metres tall and weighing 25 tonnes each. They have been there for around 5,000 years, older than the pyramids of Egypt. The bluestones (the smaller inner stones) were somehow hauled all the way from Wales, more than 250km away, using only ropes, logs and human muscle. We still do not know exactly why Stonehenge was built. The leading guesses: a giant calendar (the stones line up with the sunrise on the longest and shortest days of the year), a temple to the sun, a burial place, or all three at once. Every year on midsummer's morning, thousands of people gather to watch the sun rise through the stones.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you had to build one thing that would stand for 5,000 years, what would you want it to do for the people who found it?

stories.question: Why do Brits drive on the left?
stories.body: About a third of the world's countries drive on the left, and most were once part of the British Empire (Australia, India, South Africa, Japan-ish). The British have done it since at least the 1700s, and possibly much earlier. The most popular theory: in the days of swords, most people were right-handed, so riders kept their sword arm closest to oncoming strangers, just in case. Riding on the left also let a horseman mount his horse (which you do from the left side) onto the road safely. Napoleon, who was left-handed, supposedly preferred the right side, and as his armies marched across Europe in the early 1800s, most countries he conquered switched to the right. Britain, which Napoleon never beat, kept driving on the left, and most of its empire kept doing the same.

stories.question: What was the Blitz?
stories.body: During World War 2, between September 1940 and May 1941, Nazi Germany bombed London almost every single night for over 8 months, trying to destroy Britain's spirit and force a surrender. Around 40,000 civilians died across the UK. Around 1.4 million Londoners lost their homes. People slept in Tube stations turned into shelters, with bunk beds lining the platforms. There were no compulsory evacuations of London. Children were sent to safety in the countryside, often to families they had never met. The Blitz did not work as a strategy: Britain did not surrender, and the country was even more determined to fight on. Today you can still find buildings in London with bomb damage left visible as a memorial.
stories.thinkingQuestion: During the Blitz, ordinary people kept going to work and school every single day, knowing they might be bombed at night. Where do you think that kind of resilience comes from?

stories.question: Why did the modern world begin in Britain?
stories.body: Between roughly 1760 and 1840, in a small island in the North Atlantic, something happened that changed the whole world. Britain invented the Industrial Revolution. People stopped making cloth by hand at home and started making it in steam-powered factories. Coal mines fed huge furnaces that produced iron. Inventors built the first steam engines, the first railways, the first cotton mills. Cities like Manchester, Birmingham and Glasgow exploded in size. Britain became the richest country in the world (powered partly by its empire and, for a long time, by the slave trade, which the country eventually banned). The cost was enormous: terrible working conditions, child labour, smoke-choked cities, polluted rivers. Almost every modern factory, train station and city in the world traces back to ideas first tried out in northern England 250 years ago.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you have eaten on this trip so far?
How many different British accents can you spot in one day?
How many kinds of weather have you had in one day? Out of 10, how dramatic is the British weather really?
If you HAD to live in just one of the four nations (England, Scotland, Wales or Northern Ireland), which one?
What is one thing about your own country that you have noticed for the first time on this trip?
The UK was once the biggest empire in history, and now is a small island again. How do you feel about that, when you look around today?

wordSearch.hint: British foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Urban fox
animals.description: In gardens, dustbins, suburban streets at night. The most British wild animal.

animals.name: Common magpie
animals.description: "One for sorrow, two for joy" — black-and-white birds you greet for luck.

animals.name: Grey squirrel
animals.description: In every park. Cheeky, fast, will steal your sandwich.

animals.name: Sheep in a field
animals.description: There are more sheep than people in Wales and parts of Scotland.

animals.name: Red or roe deer
animals.description: Roe deer slip across country roads at dawn. Red deer in Scottish glens.

animals.name: Red squirrel
animals.description: Rare now, mostly in Scotland and Anglesey. Tufty ears, ginger fur.

animals.name: Hedgehog
animals.description: Snuffling along a hedge at dusk. Numbers have crashed — celebrate every one.
